mcqs.question: Consider a singly linked list with head pointer 'head'. What is the correct sequence of operations to insert a new node with value 25 at the beginning of the list?
mcqs.explanation: First, make the new node point to the current head (the existing first node), then update head to point to the new node. This preserves the link to the rest of the list.

mcqs.question: A singly linked list has 5 nodes. What is the minimum number of pointers you need to maintain to access all nodes?
mcqs.explanation: Only the head pointer is mandatory. From any node, you can traverse to all subsequent nodes by following the next pointers.

mcqs.question: What will be the output of traversing a singly linked list starting from head until NULL is encountered?
mcqs.explanation: Linked list traversal follows the next pointers sequentially, displaying elements in forward order from head until NULL is reached.

mcqs.question: In a singly linked list, if you delete a node pointed to by 'temp', which statement is correct about freeing memory?

options.text: free(temp) after setting temp->next to NULL
Memory is automatically freed when node goes out of scope

mcqs.explanation: First save the next pointer (temp->next), then free(temp). If you free first, you lose the reference to the rest of the list.

mcqs.question: A singly linked list has nodes with integer data. If the head points to NULL (empty list), what happens when you call a function to delete the first node?

options.text: The function returns an error or does nothing

mcqs.explanation: An empty list check is mandatory. Attempting to delete from an empty list should either return an error code or handle gracefully to prevent undefined behavior.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of inserting a node at position k (0-indexed) in a singly linked list of n nodes?
mcqs.explanation: We must traverse k-1 nodes to reach position k-1 (where insertion happens), giving O(k) time. Worst case k=n gives O(n).

mcqs.question: Which of the following operations is NOT O(1) in a singly linked list?
mcqs.explanation: Finding the kth element requires traversing k nodes (or n-k from end), giving O(n) time. All others are O(1).

mcqs.question: In C, what does 'struct Node* ptr = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));' do?

options.text: Allocates memory for one node on the heap
Declares a pointer to a node

mcqs.explanation: malloc allocates bytes equal to sizeof(struct Node) from heap memory and returns a void pointer cast to struct Node*.

mcqs.question: What will happen if you forget to set the last node's next pointer to NULL when building a linked list?
mcqs.explanation: Without NULL, the traversal will continue beyond the list into undefined memory, potentially causing an infinite loop or segmentation fault.

mcqs.question: To delete a node with value X from a singly linked list, what minimum information do you need?

options.text: Pointer to the node to delete
Pointer to node before the one to delete
All node pointers in the list

mcqs.explanation: Since nodes only have next pointers, you need the previous node to update its next pointer to skip the node being deleted. With only the node itself, you cannot access the previous node.

mcqs.question: A singly linked list is represented as: HEAD → 10 → 20 → 30 → NULL. After inserting 15 after node 20, what is the correct order?
mcqs.explanation: Inserting after 20: 20->next should point to new node (15), and 15->next should point to 30. The list becomes 10→20→15→30→NULL.

mcqs.question: What is the space overhead per node in a singly linked list compared to an array of n integers?

options.text: O(1) extra per node for the pointer
O(n) extra total for all pointers
No overhead, array uses more space

mcqs.explanation: Each node stores one additional pointer (4-8 bytes depending on system), giving O(1) space overhead per node compared to storing just data.

mcqs.question: Which scenario makes singly linked lists more efficient than arrays for insertions?

options.text: Inserting at the middle of the list
Inserting at the beginning of the list
Insertions are equally efficient in both

mcqs.explanation: Array insertion at beginning requires O(n) to shift all elements. Linked list insertion at beginning is O(1) - just update one pointer.

mcqs.question: What is the worst-case time complexity to count the number of nodes in a singly linked list?
mcqs.explanation: Counting requires visiting every node once, giving O(n) time complexity.

mcqs.question: In a singly linked list with 100 nodes, if you have a pointer to the 50th node, how do you delete it efficiently?

options.text: Simply call free() on the pointer
Copy data from next node, then delete next node
Must traverse from head to find previous node

mcqs.explanation: Without a previous pointer, you cannot directly delete. The workaround is to copy the next node's data into current node, then delete the next node.
